This gap limits our ability to design conservation strategies that support multi-trophic biodiversity, a core goal in landscape ecology and applied conservation.
proposals.hypothesis: The positive effect of landscape-scale habitat amount on species occurrence and abundance scales predictably from plants to higher trophic levels, but the influence of habitat configuration (e.g., patch size, connectivity) is trophic-level dependent, with stronger effects on mobile animals than on plants.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-region, multi-taxa field study across agricultural-forest mosaics in eastern Canada, surveying vascular plants (producers), butterflies and orthopterans (herbivores), and birds (predators/insectivores). Using pre-2026 satellite imagery and land cover data, we will calculate landscape metrics (habitat amount, mean patch size, inter-patch distance) at multiple spatial scales (250m to 2km radii) around each survey site. We will use generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) with region as a random effect to test for interactions between trophic level, habitat amount, and configuration metrics on species richness and abundance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of how landscape heterogeneity effects scale across trophic levels, directly informing the 'habitat amount hypothesis' in a multi-trophic context. It will generate practical guidance on whether landscape management for a flagship species (e.g., monarch butterfly) can be expected to co-benefit species at other trophic levels, or if trophic-specific configuration targets are needed.
proposals.novelty: While Fahrig's work has established the primacy of habitat amount over configuration for many species, applying this framework explicitly across trophic levels to see if the 'rules' change is novel. It integrates her core themes of scale, habitat, and species effects into a unified multi-trophic test, moving beyond single-taxon or single-trophic-level studies.

proposals.title: Integrating Land Cost and Habitat Configuration to Optimize Conservation Outcomes for Mobile Species
proposals.motivation: Classical reserve design models often assume a fixed total area for conservation, an unrealistic constraint that ignores how land cost varies spatially and how configuration influences ecological effectiveness. This disconnect limits the practical application of spatial ecology to real-world conservation planning, where budgets are fixed but the area and arrangement of habitat that can be secured are not. Integrating economic and ecological trade-offs is crucial for actionable plans, especially for species like the monarch butterfly, where road mortality and patch configuration are key population drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given conservation budget, optimizing both the spatial configuration of reserves and the total area conserved—by explicitly incorporating spatially variable land costs—will yield significantly higher predicted population persistence for target species than optimization approaches constrained by a fixed total area.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit, cost-constrained optimization model using mixed integer programming. The model will use pre-2026 metapopulation models (e.g., incidence function models) for species like the eastern migratory monarch butterfly, parameterized with data on dispersal and habitat quality. Land cost layers will be derived from agricultural land value data. The algorithm will maximize a population viability metric subject to a budget constraint, allowing it to trade off between purchasing fewer, high-quality/well-connected patches versus more, cheaper but potentially fragmented habitat. Scenario analyses will compare outcomes against fixed-area benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework that bridges spatial ecology and conservation economics, moving beyond the fixed-area paradigm to produce more cost-effective reserve designs. It will offer concrete, scalable methods for agencies to maximize ecological returns on conservation investments, directly applicable to pressing recovery targets for species like the monarch butterfly.
proposals.novelty: While cost has been included in reserve selection, the joint, flexible optimization of total area and configuration under a budget constraint for metapopulation viability represents a significant conceptual and methodological advance over standard fixed-area or fixed-number-of-patches approaches in spatial ecology.

proposals.title: The role of matrix permeability and quality variation in determining the effectiveness of habitat networks for terrestrial species
proposals.motivation: Conservation planning often treats the landscape as binary patches of habitat and non-habitat, ignoring the functional quality of the intervening matrix. This simplification is problematic because species like the monarch butterfly interact with the entire landscape; the matrix provides resources, movement corridors, and mortality risks (e.g., roadkill). Understanding how variation in matrix quality influences population persistence is crucial for designing effective reserve networks and landscape-scale conservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the population-level benefits of increasing habitat amount and connectivity are strongly mediated by the permeability and resource quality of the anthropogenic matrix, such that a high-quality matrix can compensate for lower habitat area, while a low-quality matrix negates connectivity gains.
proposals.method: We will develop spatially explicit, individual-based population models for a suite of species with different dispersal abilities and matrix dependencies (e.g., forest birds, butterflies). The landscape will be represented as a raster where each cell has a habitat suitability value derived from land cover, road density, and resource maps. We will systematically vary the configuration of high-quality habitat patches and the average permeability/quality of the matrix cells. Model simulations will run for 100 years, with population size and persistence as key response variables. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models to partition the effects of habitat amount, configuration, and matrix quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for integrating matrix quality into conservation planning, moving beyond the patch-matrix paradigm. It will generate specific, testable predictions about when improving matrix permeability is a more effective conservation action than adding or connecting habitat patches. The results will directly inform policies like the trinational monarch recovery target by evaluating the efficacy of roadside milkweed planting versus protected area expansion.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of matrix quality is acknowledged, its explicit, systematic variation and interaction with classic patch-based metrics in population models is not standard. This study directly tests the assumption that matrix variation can fundamentally alter the scale-dependent relationships between habitat pattern and population response, a core theme in Fahrig's work.

proposals.title: Evolutionary rescue in fragmented landscapes: Modeling adaptive trait changes in response to reserve network configuration
proposals.motivation: Habitat fragmentation is a primary driver of biodiversity loss, but most conservation models treat species as static entities. Pre-2026 work, including Fahrig's own, shows that population persistence depends on the interplay of habitat amount, configuration, and species traits. However, the potential for rapid, adaptive evolution in response to fragmentation itself—a process of evolutionary rescue—remains poorly integrated into spatial conservation planning, creating uncertainty in long-term projections for species like the migratory monarch butterfly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating trait evolution into spatially explicit population models will reveal non-linear thresholds in reserve network design, where specific configurations of patch size and connectivity either facilitate or hinder adaptive responses to fragmentation, ultimately determining long-term metapopulation viability.
proposals.method: We will develop an individual-based model (IBM) that couples a spatially explicit landscape of habitat patches with a quantitative genetics module. The model will simulate populations (e.g., of a generic forest insect or bird) where key dispersal-related traits (e.g., dispersal distance, edge permeability) are heritable and under selection pressure from the fragmented matrix. We will use the model to run simulation experiments, systematically varying habitat amount, patch configuration, and selection strength, measuring resulting trait evolution and population persistence over 100-500 generations. Statistical analysis will identify landscape metrics that best predict evolutionary outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for evaluating the evolutionary consequences of reserve design, moving beyond demographic snapshots. It will directly inform conservation strategies by identifying landscape configurations that promote adaptive capacity, thereby contributing to more resilient protected area networks under environmental change.
proposals.novelty: While evolutionary rescue is studied in non-spatial contexts, and spatial ecology models fragmentation effects, their integration is rare. This proposal uniquely applies quantitative genetics within a spatially explicit, individual-based metapopulation model—a synthesis not common in pre-2026 landscape ecology—to ask how reserve design itself shapes adaptive trajectories.

proposals.title: Empirical validation of the SLOSS trade-off: Testing predictions of species diversity in fragmented forest reserve networks
proposals.motivation: The Single Large Or Several Small (SLOSS) debate remains a central, unresolved question in conservation planning, with profound implications for habitat protection strategies. Theoretical models predict trade-offs in species diversity based on reserve size and configuration, but these predictions lack rigorous, large-scale empirical validation in real-world landscapes. This gap limits our ability to design effective reserve networks, particularly for species with varying habitat and dispersal requirements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for forest-dwelling species, the relationship between total species richness and reserve network configuration (one large vs. several small reserves of equal total area) is scale-dependent and varies predictably with species traits, specifically dispersal ability and habitat specialization.
proposals.method: We will use a space-for-time substitution across multiple fragmented forest landscapes in eastern North America. Species occurrence data for birds, plants, and arthropods will be compiled from existing databases and augmented with field surveys. For each landscape, we will quantify the observed species richness in real-world reserve networks that approximate 'single large' and 'several small' configurations. We will test model predictions using generalized linear mixed models, with species richness as the response and configuration, spatial scale, and species trait interactions as predictors, while controlling for landscape context variables like matrix quality and connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, multi-taxa test of SLOSS model predictions, moving the debate from theory to evidence-based guidance. It will directly inform conservation policy by identifying the conditions under which one reserve design strategy is likely to outperform another for preserving biodiversity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, trait-based empirical testing of scale-dependent SLOSS predictions across real reserve networks, integrating Fahrig's work on the scale-dependence of habitat effects with contemporary fragmentation theory, rather than relying on simulated landscapes or single-species models.

proposals.title: Optimizing habitat restoration for migratory species under realistic economic and land-use constraints: A case study with the eastern monarch butterfly
proposals.motivation: Conservation planning models often identify optimal habitat configurations based solely on ecological criteria, ignoring the economic and land-use realities that determine implementation feasibility. This gap is critical for species like the eastern migratory monarch butterfly, where recovery targets require substantial new milkweed habitat in working agricultural landscapes. Pre-2026 work by Fahrig and others has quantified habitat needs and threats like roadkill, but has not formally integrated the costs and landowner willingness that ultimately constrain restoration.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating explicit economic (e.g., land rental costs, program budgets) and land-use (e.g., crop type suitability, parcel size) constraints into reserve optimization models will significantly alter the predicted spatial configuration and efficiency of habitat networks needed to achieve population recovery targets.
proposals.method: We will extend Fahrig's existing spatial optimization framework for milkweed habitat by integrating a constrained optimization algorithm, such as integer linear programming or a heuristic like simulated annealing. The model will be parameterized with spatially explicit data on land opportunity costs (e.g., from agricultural census data), landowner enrollment probability (from survey data), and a fixed budget or area constraint. We will apply this to the Canadian portion of the monarch's range, comparing optimal solutions from the constrained model to those from the purely ecological model to evaluate trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic and actionable template for conservation planning that bridges ecological population targets and socio-economic feasibility. It will directly inform the implementation of Canada's trinational monarch recovery plan by identifying priority regions where habitat gains are both ecologically effective and economically viable. The methodological framework will be transferable to other landscape-scale conservation challenges.
proposals.novelty: While economic constraints are used in general reserve design, their explicit integration into Fahrig's scale-sensitive, population-focused models for specific migratory species is novel. This moves beyond identifying 'where habitat should be' to 'where habitat can feasibly be placed given real-world constraints,' a crucial step for applied conservation.

proposals.title: Scaling up from patches to landscapes: Integrating satellite-derived environmental gradients and fragmentation metrics to improve population models for monarch butterflies
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in landscape ecology is accurately scaling habitat effects on populations from local patches to entire migratory ranges. Pre-2026 models often rely on simplified, static habitat maps, failing to capture real-world environmental gradients and fine-scale fragmentation that critically affect species like the migratory monarch butterfly. This gap limits our ability to predict population responses to land-use change and to design effective, large-scale conservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that population models for migratory monarch butterflies parameterized with high-resolution, time-series satellite data on resource gradients (e.g., nectar availability, milkweed density) and fragmentation metrics will more accurately predict population trends and identify key limiting factors across spatial scales than models using traditional, categorical land-cover maps.
proposals.method: We will use Sentinel-2 and Landsat 8 imagery to derive continuous, seasonal metrics of vegetation productivity (NDVI, EVI) and flowering phenology as proxies for nectar and host plant resources across the eastern North American breeding range. Fragmentation will be quantified using moving-window metrics (e.g., edge density, patch cohesion) on classified habitat layers. These dynamic, gridded covariates will be integrated into spatially explicit population models, linking resource availability and configuration to monarch demographic rates estimated from existing survey data (e.g., Journey North, summer breeding counts).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, transferable framework for moving beyond patch-based models to gradient-based, landscape-scale population projections. It will directly inform the trinational monarch recovery plan by identifying not just *how much* habitat is needed, but *how* it should be configured in real, heterogeneous landscapes to support population recovery.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing is commonly used for habitat mapping, its novel integration here lies in using continuous spectral indices to parameterize *resource gradients* directly in demographic models, moving beyond the assumption of habitat as a binary variable and explicitly testing how the scaling of fragmentation effects influences population outcomes.

proposals.title: Scaling landscape ecology theory to microbes: Testing the reserve size-fragmentation framework in bacterial metacommunities
proposals.motivation: Landscape ecology's reserve size-fragmentation framework, central to conservation planning for macro-organisms, has never been rigorously tested at microbial scales. Microbial communities in patchy environments like soil aggregates, plant leaves, or medical biofilms form metacommunities, but it is unknown if classic trade-offs between patch size and isolation govern their diversity and function. Understanding this is crucial for predicting microbial-driven processes in fragmented habitats, from soil carbon cycling to pathogen spread in hospitals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for bacterial metacommunities, the relationship between 'habitat' patch size, inter-patch distance, and community diversity will follow predictions from macro-ecology, but with the strength of these effects modulated by microbial dispersal capacity and generation time.
proposals.method: We will create a controlled microbial landscape using microfluidic chips (e.g., Soil Chips) or arrays of microwells to define patches of varying size and spatial isolation. We will inoculate a diverse bacterial consortium from soil into a source patch and track metacommunity assembly over time via time-lapse microscopy and 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing. We will quantify diversity (alpha, beta), composition, and function (e.g., substrate degradation). Effects will be analyzed using generalized linear mixed models, with patch size, isolation, and their interaction as fixed effects, testing against null models of random assembly.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of foundational landscape ecology theory in a microbial system, potentially unifying principles across scales. It will generate novel predictive frameworks for microbial ecology in fragmented environments. The results could inform bioengineering of microbial consortia and the management of microbiomes in agricultural or clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious application is that high dispersal rates and short generation times of microbes may decouple diversity from spatial structure, challenging macro-ecological theory. Explicitly testing the scale-dependence of the fragmentation paradigm at the microbial level is a novel synthesis of Fahrig's spatial ecology with contemporary microbial ecology.

proposals.title: Integrating landscape complementation and road mortality risk into a continental-scale corridor network for migratory monarch butterflies
proposals.motivation: Continental-scale conservation for migratory species like the monarch butterfly is hindered by piecemeal, patch-focused planning that fails to account for the functional connectivity of the entire migratory route. Pre-2026 research, including Fahrig's work, highlights that roadkill is a major population driver and that habitat needs (e.g., milkweed for breeding, nectar for fueling) are spatially separated. A continental plan must explicitly link habitat patches while mitigating road mortality to support the trinational recovery target.
proposals.hypothesis: A continental conservation network designed using principles of landscape complementation and mortality risk minimization will identify a more efficient and viable corridor configuration than networks designed solely on habitat patch size or proximity, leading to higher projected population persistence.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale, graph-theoretic model integrating pre-2026 land cover data (e.g., Cropland Data Layer, forest maps) and road networks across North America. Nodes will be habitat patches (milkweed sites, nectar corridors) weighted by quality. Edges will be weighted by a composite cost function incorporating Euclidean distance, landscape resistance (from land cover), and a road mortality risk layer derived from traffic volume and known monarch roadkill hotspots. We will use a modified minimum Steiner tree algorithm and circuit theory (Circuitscape) to identify optimal continental linkages, comparing scenarios that prioritize different cost components.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first operational framework for designing a continental wildlife corridor that explicitly balances habitat acquisition with mortality mitigation. It will advance landscape ecology by scaling Fahrig's 'habitat amount' and road effect theories to a migratory system, offering a testable, map-based plan for trinational monarch recovery.
proposals.novelty: While corridor models exist, none have integrated landscape complementation (breeding vs. fueling habitats) with continental-scale mortality sinks (roads) into a single optimization for a migratory insect, moving beyond simple structural connectivity to model functional, demographic connectivity.

proposals.title: A multi-scale, dynamic framework to unify landscape and subcommunity processes for predicting population persistence
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in landscape ecology is that population responses to habitat loss and fragmentation are scale-dependent, and static models fail to capture dynamic processes like climate-driven range shifts or metapopulation turnover. This disconnect limits unified predictions, as seen in the uncertainty of projecting Arctic shorebird habitat shifts and the complex drivers of monarch butterfly declines, which involve roadkill (local scale), milkweed availability (landscape scale), and continental migration (broad scale).
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating landscape-scale habitat configuration with subcommunity-scale species interactions and dispersal dynamics into a single, hierarchical model will significantly improve the accuracy and generality of predictions for population persistence across taxa and systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian state-space model. The landscape layer will use circuit theory or graph-based metrics to quantify functional connectivity from land-cover data. The subcommunity layer will incorporate dynamic occupancy models or integral projection models (IPMs) parameterized with field data on demography and local interactions. These layers will be coupled via dispersal kernels that are functions of landscape resistance. The model will be fitted using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, allowing for simultaneous estimation of parameters across scales. We will test it using existing long-term data on forest songbird and butterfly populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable modeling framework that explicitly links pattern (landscape structure) with process (dispersal, demography) across scales. It will advance the theoretical integration of landscape ecology and population biology, offering conservation planners a more robust tool for predicting species responses to combined threats like habitat fragmentation and climate change.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale analyses are common, simultaneously fitting a unified model that dynamically couples broad-scale connectivity with fine-scale population processes in a hierarchical Bayesian framework is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond correlative, single-scale habitat models or purely theoretical metapopulation constructs.

proposals.title: Evaluating the efficacy of roadside habitat restoration versus protected-area expansion for monarch butterfly recovery using a spatially explicit population model
proposals.motivation: Conservation planning for migratory species like the eastern monarch butterfly faces acute trade-offs. Pre-2026 research, including Fahrig's, identifies road mortality as a major population threat, while habitat restoration targets focus on adding milkweed stems. It is unclear whether adding habitat in high-mortality roadside corridors or in safer, consolidated patches yields greater population benefits, creating a critical uncertainty for national and trinational recovery plans.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given amount of new milkweed habitat, a spatially explicit model will show that strategic placement to minimize roadkill mortality will produce a greater increase in the breeding population size than placement based solely on habitat suitability or existing protected area networks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a spatially explicit, individual-based population model, parameterized with pre-2026 data on monarch demography and movement. The model landscape will represent a real agricultural region in Ontario, Canada, integrating GIS layers for roads, land cover, and existing protected areas. We will simulate multiple conservation scenarios: adding milkweed randomly, within protected area buffers, and specifically in roadside right-of-ways versus in low-traffic agricultural fields. Model evaluation will compare end-of-season population size and network connectivity metrics across scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, scenario-based tool for conservation agencies to prioritize habitat restoration locations, directly informing Canada's contribution to the trinational monarch recovery target. It will advance the integration of road ecology and habitat fragmentation theory into practical spatial planning, moving beyond simple habitat-area targets.
proposals.novelty: While habitat models are common, applying a spatially explicit demographic framework to directly compare the population consequences of habitat placement strategies in a real-world, road-affected landscape is novel. It tests the non-obvious premise that avoiding mortality sources may be more impactful than maximizing habitat patch size or quality alone.

proposals.title: Species-Specific Habitat Definition Resolves Ambiguity in Tests of the Habitat Amount Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: The Habitat Amount Hypothesis (HAH) posits that species richness is determined by the total amount of habitat in a landscape, not patch size or isolation. However, empirical tests have yielded mixed results, potentially because 'habitat' is often defined using broad land-cover classes that may not reflect a species' actual resource use. This ambiguity undermines the hypothesis's predictive power and its utility for conservation planning, as seen in debates over monarch butterfly and Arctic shorebird habitat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tests of the HAH will be more consistent and predictive when habitat is defined using species-specific, resource-based criteria rather than generic land-cover categories.
proposals.method: We will develop species-specific habitat definitions for a terrestrial (e.g., monarch butterfly) and a wetland bird (e.g., a shorebird) using pre-2026 species distribution models and resource selection functions. For each species, we will re-analyze existing datasets on species occurrence and landscape composition. We will compare the explanatory power of the HAH when habitat is defined generically (e.g., 'grassland' or 'wetland') versus our species-specific definition, using generalized linear mixed models and multi-model inference within an information-theoretic framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, operational framework for defining habitat in landscape ecology, directly addressing a core ambiguity in the HAH literature. It will advance the field by demonstrating how species-specific resource needs must be integrated into landscape-scale hypotheses, leading to more reliable predictions for conservation, such as habitat restoration targets for species at risk.
proposals.novelty: While the need for better habitat definitions is acknowledged, this study explicitly links the methodological choice of habitat definition to the outcome of testing a major, contentious ecological hypothesis, moving beyond correlation to causal inference in landscape-scale studies.

proposals.title: Mapping Habitat Amount Gradients with Geostatistics: A Continuous-Field Approach to Quantifying Landscape Effects on Species
proposals.motivation: A central tenet in landscape ecology is the habitat amount hypothesis, which posits that species occurrence is driven by the total amount of habitat in the local landscape, not patch size or isolation. However, empirical tests often rely on coarse, categorical land-cover maps, which misrepresent the continuous nature of habitat suitability and introduce uncertainty into projections of species responses to habitat change. A method to quantify habitat amount as a spatially continuous gradient is critically needed to improve the realism and predictive power of landscape-scale models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using geostatistical interpolation to model habitat amount as a continuous spatial field will explain more variance in species occurrence data than traditional binary or categorical habitat maps, particularly for species that perceive habitat as a gradient.
proposals.method: We will apply ordinary kriging and regression kriging to point-sampled habitat quality data (e.g., milkweed stem density for monarch butterflies) to generate continuous, fine-resolution maps of habitat amount across study landscapes. These geostatistical surfaces will serve as predictor variables in species distribution models (e.g., generalized linear mixed models) for focal species, which will be compared against models using traditional patch-based or categorical habitat metrics. The analysis will leverage existing spatial and temporal data on species populations from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for quantifying the habitat amount hypothesis using continuous gradients, moving beyond the patch-matrix paradigm. It will directly test whether a geostatistical representation of habitat improves model fit and reduces projection uncertainty for species of conservation concern, such as the migratory monarch butterfly, thereby offering more reliable tools for habitat recovery planning.
proposals.novelty: While geostatistics is well-established in environmental sciences, its application to explicitly model and test the habitat amount hypothesis as a continuous field is novel. This approach bridges a gap between Fahrig's conceptual framework and advanced spatial statistical methods rarely combined in landscape ecology.

proposals.title: Testing the Habitat Amount Hypothesis at the Microscale: A Microbial Metacommunity Experiment
proposals.motivation: The Habitat Amount Hypothesis (HAH), a cornerstone of landscape ecology, posits that species richness is determined by the total amount of habitat in a landscape, not by patch size or isolation. While supported at coarse scales, its universality across scales remains debated. Testing it at very fine, controlled scales is crucial to isolate its core mechanisms from confounding factors like dispersal limitation and matrix quality, which are pronounced in macro-organisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the HAH will hold for microbial communities in a microlandscape, where dispersal is effectively unlimited, such that species richness and composition are a function of total habitat amount, independent of the specific configuration of habitat patches.
proposals.method: We will construct microcosm landscapes using agar plates as the matrix and nutrient-rich agar patches as habitat. We will manipulate total habitat amount (e.g., 10%, 30%, 50% cover) and configuration (few large patches vs. many small patches) while holding amount constant. A diverse, defined bacterial community will be inoculated. Using high-throughput sequencing (16S rRNA) and automated image analysis, we will track population dynamics and community composition across space and time. Statistical analysis will employ generalized linear mixed models to partition variance explained by habitat amount versus configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, replicated test of the HAH in a system where dispersal is non-limiting, isolating the role of habitat amount per se. It will bridge landscape ecology with microbial ecology, offering a tractable model for metacommunity theory. The results will clarify the scale-dependency of the HAH, informing its application in conservation planning for fragmented systems.
proposals.novelty: Applying the HAH framework to microbial metacommunities at the centimeter scale is a novel synthesis of macro-ecological theory and micro-ecological experimentation. It directly tests a fundamental but contested assumption of the HAH—that it operates independently of configuration when dispersal is not a constraint—in a way impossible with larger organisms.

proposals.title: Inverting the Species-Area Relationship: A Predictive Framework for Habitat Amount Targets Based on Desired Species Richness
proposals.motivation: Conservation planning and policy, such as the trinational monarch recovery target, require clear, defensible habitat amount goals. While the positive relationship between habitat amount and species richness is well-established, it is typically used descriptively. An inverse, predictive framework to determine the habitat amount required to sustain a target richness is critically needed to translate biodiversity objectives into actionable landscape-scale conservation targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a known species-area relationship (SAR) for a landscape and taxonomic group, the minimum habitat amount required to sustain a target level of species richness can be reliably predicted by inverting the SAR function, with prediction uncertainty quantifiable from the variance in SAR parameters.
proposals.method: We will compile empirical datasets of species richness (e.g., birds, plants, arthropods) across forest and grassland patches of varying sizes from pre-2026 literature and databases. For each system, we will fit multiple candidate SAR models (e.g., power, logarithmic) using maximum likelihood estimation. The inverse function of the best-fit model will be derived algebraically. We will then develop a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework to propagate parameter uncertainty from the fitted SAR into prediction intervals for the required habitat amount, explicitly testing the model's predictive accuracy using cross-validation on held-out data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for setting habitat conservation targets based on explicit biodiversity outcomes, moving beyond rules-of-thumb. It will directly inform policy by offering a science-based method to calculate habitat needs, such as the milkweed stems required for monarchs, while explicitly communicating the associated uncertainty to decision-makers.
proposals.novelty: While SARs are foundational in ecology, their explicit inversion to solve for habitat area as a function of target richness is a non-trivial, underutilized application. This approach formally bridges descriptive landscape ecology and prescriptive conservation planning in a statistically rigorous way, addressing a core gap in Fahrig's habitat amount hypothesis by making it operational for target-setting.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Two-Unit Management Strategy for Asian Elephant Conservation in China: Integrating Habitat Connectivity, Population Viability, and Road Mortality Risk
proposals.motivation: The fragmented populations of Asian elephants in China's Yunnan province face critical threats from habitat loss and increasing human-elephant conflict. A proposed two-unit management strategy, separating the Nangunhe population from a combined Mengla/Shangyong (MSJ/SY) unit, requires rigorous evaluation to determine if it effectively balances species recovery goals with human safety and landscape constraints, a classic conservation planning problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that managing the Nangunhe and combined MSJ/SY populations as separate units, with targeted habitat restoration and mitigation of linear infrastructure, will result in higher overall metapopulation viability and reduced road mortality compared to a scenario treating the region as a single management unit.
proposals.method: We will apply a spatially explicit population viability analysis (PVA) framework. Using pre-2026 satellite imagery and land cover data, we will map habitat patches and quality for each proposed unit. Circuit theory models (e.g., using Circuitscape) will quantify functional connectivity and identify key corridors and barriers. We will integrate known elephant movement data and road networks to model roadkill risk, adapting methods from Fahrig's work on monarch butterflies. Population dynamics will be simulated across multiple spatial and temporal scales to compare viability metrics under the two-unit versus single-unit scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, evidence-based framework for implementing the two-unit management strategy, directly informing Chinese conservation policy. It will advance the application of landscape-scale, multi-threat population models to large mammal conservation, explicitly integrating road mortality—a growing source of anthropogenic additive mortality—into PVA for fragmented populations.
proposals.novelty: While habitat connectivity for Asian elephants has been studied, this proposal novelly integrates explicit road mortality risk and population viability modeling at the management unit scale, applying a landscape ecology perspective typically used for smaller species to a large, wide-ranging mammal in a complex human-dominated matrix.

proposals.title: Testing Extinction Thresholds in Fragmented Landscapes: An Empirical Comparison of Continuous-Edge and BIDE Model Predictions for Forest-Dependent Species
proposals.motivation: Predicting species persistence in fragmented habitats is central to conservation planning. Two dominant model types, Continuous-Edge (CE) models and Birth-Immigration-Death-Emigration (BIDE) models, make contrasting predictions about how extinction thresholds shift with habitat loss: CE models predict large, abrupt shifts, while BIDE models predict smaller, more gradual shifts. Resolving this theoretical discrepancy with empirical data is crucial for accurately forecasting population declines and setting effective habitat retention targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that empirical extinction thresholds in response to habitat loss will be more gradual and occur at lower levels of habitat loss than predicted by classic CE models, aligning more closely with predictions from spatially explicit BIDE models that incorporate demographic stochasticity and dispersal.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-species, multi-region field study monitoring population dynamics of forest-dependent songbirds and insects across replicated landscapes with a gradient of forest cover (5% to 60%). We will use mark-recapture and territory mapping over three breeding seasons to parameterize species-specific BIDE models. These empirical BIDE models will be compared against predictions from CE models fitted to the same landscape and occupancy data. The key test is comparing the predicted habitat amount at which the population growth rate (λ) falls below 1.0 for each model type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first direct empirical tests of a core theoretical debate in landscape ecology. It will clarify which modeling framework more accurately predicts extinction risks, thereby improving the scientific basis for habitat-based recovery targets, such as those for monarch butterflies. The results will inform the scale and configuration of habitat conservation and restoration.
proposals.novelty: While the CE vs. BIDE debate is well-established in theory, rigorous field tests are rare. This study's novelty lies in its direct empirical parameterization of both model types from the same field data, moving beyond simulation to test predictions against observed population trajectories in real fragmented landscapes.

proposals.title: Inverting the Habitat-Fragmentation Paradigm: A Trait-Based Optimization Framework for Designing Landscapes that Minimize Extinction Risk
proposals.motivation: Conservation planning typically identifies where to protect existing habitat, but rarely provides a generalizable, quantitative method to design new or restored landscapes for species persistence. Given accelerating land-use change and climate shifts, a predictive framework to solve for optimal habitat configurations from species' traits is urgently needed. This directly addresses recovery targets, like those for the monarch butterfly, by moving from descriptive models to prescriptive landscape design.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given species, defined by its dispersal ability, reproductive rate, and habitat specificity, there exists a theoretically optimal, and often non-intuitive, configuration of habitat amount and spatial arrangement that minimizes long-term extinction risk in dynamic landscapes.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse modeling framework using individual-based models (IBMs) and genetic algorithms. First, we will parameterize IBMs for species archetypes (e.g., forest specialists, grassland generalists) using pre-2026 trait databases. A genetic algorithm will then iteratively modify simulated landscape structure (patch size, number, connectivity, matrix quality) within the IBM to minimize a quasi-extinction risk metric over 100-year simulations. The optimization will be constrained by total habitat budget, mimicking real-world restoration limits. We will validate outputs using existing long-term data on species like the monarch and Arctic shorebirds.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first general, trait-based optimization tool for designing persistence-optimal landscapes, shifting the field from pattern analysis to solution generation. It will provide concrete, testable configurations for habitat restoration that balance amount, arrangement, and matrix effects, directly informing policy like the trinational monarch recovery plan.
proposals.novelty: While landscape ecology excels at predicting effects of configuration on populations, the inverse problem—solving for configuration from traits—is largely unaddressed. This approach integrates Fahrig's insights on habitat amount and configuration with optimization algorithms from engineering, creating a novel synthesis for conservation design.

proposals.title: A multi-taxa, multi-guild meta-analysis of landscape-scale habitat effects on population persistence
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses in landscape ecology have historically been biased towards well-studied taxa (e.g., birds, forest mammals) and specific trophic levels, limiting the generality of conclusions about habitat amount and configuration effects. This taxonomic and functional bias, evident in pre-2026 syntheses, obscures whether established scaling relationships and fragmentation thresholds apply broadly or are taxon-specific. A more comprehensive synthesis is urgently needed to inform effective, multi-species conservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect sizes of habitat amount and configuration on population responses are not universal but vary systematically across taxonomic groups and trophic guilds, with under-represented groups (e.g., invertebrates, herpetofauna, lower trophic levels) showing stronger or more scale-dependent responses.
proposals.method: We will systematically expand an existing meta-analysis database by searching for studies on under-represented taxa (arthropods, amphibians, reptiles, non-forest plants) and guilds (e.g., pollinators, detritivores, primary producers). For each study, we will extract or calculate standardized effect sizes (e.g., correlation coefficients, log response ratios) linking population metrics to habitat predictors at multiple spatial scales. We will use multi-level meta-regression models (restricted maximum likelihood estimation) with taxonomic group, trophic level, and habitat type as moderators, while controlling for study methodology and spatial grain.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively robust, cross-taxonomic assessment of habitat effects, directly testing the generality of the habitat amount hypothesis. It will identify which ecological guilds are most sensitive to habitat configuration versus amount, providing a stronger evidence base for predicting biodiversity responses to land-use change and for designing habitat networks that support diverse species assemblages.
proposals.novelty: While previous syntheses have noted knowledge gaps, this proposal systematically targets those gaps to explicitly test for taxonomic and functional heterogeneity in meta-analytic models, moving beyond documenting bias to quantifying its consequences for ecological theory and conservation practice.

ideas.title: Quantifying Researcher Bias in Landscape Resistance Inference Using Experimental Crowdsourcing
ideas.description: This research fuses the validation of habitat resistance models with controlled experiments on researcher inference bias. It designs a crowdsourcing experiment where multiple researchers, with varied backgrounds, are given the same empirical animal movement or genetic data and a habitat suitability model. Their task is to infer resistance values, but the experiment controls for ambiguity by using simulated landscapes where the 'true' mechanistic drivers of movement are known. By analyzing how researcher background correlates with their inferred resistance surfaces and their divergence from the known truth, the study quantifies the magnitude and nature of bias in ecological inference. This provides a method to calibrate not just the models, but the modelers, leading to more objective and reproducible landscape connectivity science.

ideas.title: A Framework for Multi-Mechanism Inference Under the Axiom of Mechanism Interaction
ideas.description: This research develops a formal methodological framework for environmental and ecological impact studies, grounded in the axiom that observed effects are always the product of interacting mechanisms (e.g., habitat fragmentation, pollution, noise, and edge effects acting in concert). The framework provides structured procedures for researchers to first generate a comprehensive set of plausible, interacting mechanisms consistent with their data and study design. It then guides them in specifying testable models of how these mechanisms combine—additively, synergistically, or antagonistically—and provides statistical tools to infer the strength and form of these interactions from empirical data, thereby challenging reductive, single-mechanism explanations.

ideas.title: Apply Econometric Instrumental Variable and Path Analysis to Disentangle Ecological Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal inference methods from econometrics, a field that routinely disentangles complex causal pathways from observational data. The analogous solution involves using instrumental variables to establish causality and structural equation modeling to quantify direct and indirect effects. Adapted to the ecological context, the research would identify a plausible instrumental variable for habitat connectivity to isolate its causal role. Subsequently, a path analysis model would be specified and fitted, using causal diagrams as a blueprint, to estimate the strength of the three primary pathways—direct mortality, connectivity-mediated, and habitat quality-mediated—through which landscape fragmentation impacts species survival. This provides a rigorous quantitative framework for testing mechanistic hypotheses in conservation biology.

ideas.title: A Bayesian meta-framework to audit and recalibrate historical connectivity models for bias
ideas.description: This research develops a Bayesian belief network (BBN) framework specifically designed for the meta-analysis of past connectivity and mitigation studies. It formalizes the review of historical expert-derived resistance values by modeling potential author bias (e.g., towards habitat quality over structural connectivity) as latent parent nodes within the BBN. This allows researchers to systematically recalibrate old models with new data and explicitly estimate how the original authors' methodological preferences may have skewed past recommendations. The outcome is a quantitative audit trail for conservation science, enabling more robust, evidence-based updates to legacy mitigation plans.

ideas.title: Individual-to-Global Mechanistic Framework: Using Intensive Tracking to Parameterize and Validate Predictive Meta-Analytic Models
ideas.description: This research program establishes a closed-loop framework that iteratively links intensive individual tracking with global meta-analysis. High-resolution data on survival, movement, and stress from tracked individuals are used to parameterize and validate mechanistic models within a global meta-analytic structure, testing whether individual-level processes scale to explain population- and community-level patterns across taxa and regions. Conversely, the meta-analytic model identifies key taxonomic, geographic, or mechanistic gaps where predictions are most uncertain, strategically guiding the deployment of new individual-based studies to collect maximally informative data. This synergy transforms both approaches from descriptive exercises into a dynamic, predictive system for understanding how road impacts emerge from individual mechanisms and vary globally.

ideas.title: Invert the mitigation recommendation bias via a 'Precautionary Principle for Mortality'
ideas.description: This idea inverts the mitigation recommendation bias by applying an analogy from environmental toxicology. In that field, the 'precautionary principle' solves the problem of uncertainty by shifting the burden of proof from proving harm to proving safety. Adapting this solution, the research proposes a 'Precautionary Principle for Mortality' in existential risk studies. The core adapted idea is to formally invert the default analytical stance: a proposed mitigation for a catastrophic AI risk should not be recommended unless proponents can demonstrate with high confidence that its primary effect is to reduce mortality or existential catastrophe probability. This embeds mortality reduction as the paramount, non-negotiable metric, systematically challenging author preferences that might prioritize other outcomes like capability growth or economic efficiency, thereby correcting the recommendation bias at the assumption level.

ideas.title: Adapting Electrical Grid Load-Flow Analysis for Dynamic Habitat Connectivity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from electrical power systems engineering, specifically dynamic load-flow analysis, to the problem of incorporating temporal dynamics into Habitat Suitability Models (HSM) for connectivity. In power grids, dynamic load-flow models use time-series data on generation and demand to simulate how power flows and network constraints evolve. Analogously, in ecology, time-series habitat suitability data (e.g., from remote sensing or climate models) can be used as dynamic inputs representing the 'generation capacity' of source patches and the 'demand' of sink patches within a circuit-theory model of the landscape. The landscape resistance matrix is treated as a dynamic impedance layer that changes with habitat conditions. By running a temporal sequence of 'ecological circuit' simulations, one can derive dynamic resistance values and map how connectivity pathways, their capacity, and critical pinch-points shift over daily, seasonal, or decadal timescales, providing a more mechanistic and predictive model for conservation planning under environmental change.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Meta-Analysis Framework for Global Mechanism Prevalence
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology to the problem of scaling up to a global meta-analysis of ecological impact mechanism prevalence. The analogous problem—estimating the global prevalence of a disease strain from heterogeneous local studies—is solved using hierarchical Bayesian meta-analysis models that account for population and methodological variance. The adapted idea is to conduct a global meta-analysis by building a hierarchical Bayesian model where the outcome is the reported prevalence of a given impact mechanism (e.g., trophic cascades, competitive exclusion). The model will have nested levels for taxonomic groups and geographic regions, with study design and quality metrics as covariates. This approach will generate robust, pooled estimates of the true prevalence of each mechanism worldwide, quantify the proportion of variance attributable to biological versus geographic differences, and adjust for systematic biases introduced by different research methodologies, thereby achieving a dual scaling in both taxonomic and geographic dimensions.

ideas.title: Treat confounded field studies as observational data for causal inference modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Epidemiology, where researchers cannot run randomized controlled trials for many exposures (e.g., long-term pollution effects) and must work with confounded observational data. The analogous solution is to employ formal causal inference methodologies—such as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to map assumed relationships, propensity score methods to balance groups, or instrumental variables to isolate variation—to estimate causal effects despite the confounding. Adapted to road ecology, this means relaxing the assumption that field study designs can isolate mechanisms like noise, chemical pollution, or physical barrier effects. Instead, researchers would treat standard field studies (e.g., transects near roads) as inherently confounded observational data. The adapted research approach involves explicitly mapping the assumed confounding pathways between road presence and ecological responses, then applying causal modeling techniques (e.g., structural equation models, path analysis with measured confounders) to the existing data. This shifts the goal from definitively isolating a single mechanism to probabilistically attributing observed effects to different candidate mechanisms based on the strength of evidence within the confounded dataset, providing more realistic and nuanced conclusions about road impacts.

ideas.title: Circuit-based feedback between connectivity and habitat suitability
ideas.description: This research synthesizes circuit theory and habitat suitability modeling into a dynamic, co-evolutionary framework. It begins by using a standard HSM to generate an initial resistance surface, which is then analyzed with circuit theory to produce a detailed, probabilistic map of landscape connectivity. This connectivity map is then fed back into the HSM as a dynamic predictor variable, updating the habitat suitability predictions to reflect the positive influence of connectedness on population persistence and habitat quality. The process iterates, allowing the model to simulate how connectivity and habitat suitability influence each other over time, providing a more mechanistic and systems-level understanding of metapopulation and metacommunity dynamics under landscape change.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Sensor Calibration to Correct Author Inference Bias
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from environmental science, where data from biased sensors is calibrated against high-fidelity reference stations. The analogous problem in meta-science is author inference bias in literature reviews, where conclusions are systematically influenced by an author's theoretical leanings or methodological choices. The adapted method involves creating a benchmark 'reference review' conducted with explicit, bias-minimizing protocols for a defined research question. Using techniques from meta-research, we would then model the quantitative deviation of conclusions from other published reviews relative to this benchmark. This allows for the quantification of a 'bias signature' for different review authoring paradigms and enables the statistical correction of aggregated findings from multiple reviews, improving the objectivity of synthetic conclusions.

ideas.description: This approach first applies constraint-based causal discovery algorithms to high-resolution temporal data on species presence, movement, habitat features, and mortality to generate data-driven, testable causal hypotheses. The most salient predicted pathways are then stress-tested using designed micro-interventions or instrumental variable analysis leveraging natural policy changes or disturbances. This creates a iterative loop where the causal model is continually refined against experimental evidence, moving beyond a static, assumption-laden DAG to a dynamically validated causal understanding of the system.

ideas.title: Applying Hydraulic Circuit Theory to Ecological Connectivity Networks
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method transfer from hydrology to landscape ecology. Instead of using least-cost paths on HSM-inferred resistance surfaces, we adapt the solution from hydraulic network analysis, where circuit theory models fluid flow through pipes. Here, the landscape is conceptualized as an electrical/hydraulic circuit: high-quality habitat patches are nodes with high 'potential' (like voltage or pressure), the matrix is a resistor network with conductance values derived from HSM outputs. By applying circuit theory (Ohm's and Kirchhoff's laws), we can model the probabilistic 'flow' of organisms, genes, or ecological processes across all possible pathways. This provides a more robust measure of functional connectivity than single-path models, quantifying cumulative current, identifying critical corridors and barriers, and assessing how connectivity changes if specific landscape elements are altered or removed.

ideas.title: Quantifying Expert Reliability for Mortality-Centric Climate Mitigation Pathways
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical mitigation prioritization by explicitly treating mortality reduction as the primary objective, then systematically evaluates the conditions under which expert-derived parameters (e.g., for adaptation efficacy or health co-benefits) are reliable for modeling pathways toward that goal. It develops a diagnostic to quantify when expert judgment succeeds or fails in predicting key mortality-sensitive variables, such as the resilience of health systems or the mortality displacement of interventions. The result is a validated, mortality-optimized decision tool that filters expert input, using only the reliably quantified judgments to build robust mitigation and adaptation portfolios, thereby correcting both preference bias and reliability uncertainty simultaneously.

ideas.title: A Mechanistic Equivalence Class Framework for Transparent Inference
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) in the social sciences, where researchers routinely confront the problem of statistically equivalent causal models. The analogous solution involves formally specifying, testing, and transparently reporting all plausible models that fit the data equally well. Adapted to the original seed idea, the project will create a methodological framework to guide authors in systematically acknowledging all mechanisms consistent with their data. This 'Mechanistic Equivalence Class Framework' will provide a structured workflow for researchers to: 1) enumerate the full set of causal or explanatory mechanisms logically compatible with their evidence and design, 2) evaluate which are statistically consistent, and 3) transparently report this set in their work while discussing how auxiliary theory, future experiments, or different designs could adjudicate among them. The goal is to standardize and improve the rigor of multi-mechanism inference across empirical sciences.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography Inversion to Derive Resistance from Spatial Capture-Recapture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem framework from seismic tomography to spatial capture-recapture (SCR) analysis for resistance surface estimation. In seismology, subsurface velocity structures (resistance to seismic waves) are derived by inverting observed surface travel times. Analogously, here we propose to treat SCR encounter histories—the spatial and temporal pattern of animal detections across an array of traps or detectors—as the observed data to be inverted. An inverse model would be developed where the parameters of a resistance surface are the unknowns to be estimated. The model would work backward from the SCR-derived parameters (particularly the spatial scale of animal movement and detection probability fields) to solve for the continuous landscape resistance values that most likely generated the observed animal detection patterns. This provides a method to derive resistance values directly and quantitatively from ecological observation data, moving beyond expert opinion or generic environmental correlates.

ideas.title: Ensemble-based inference and validation of resistance landscapes from connectivity data
ideas.description: This approach reframes the ill-posed inverse problem into a robust model selection and validation challenge. It employs a multi-hypothesis framework to generate an ensemble of candidate resistance models, each representing a different biological or physical hypothesis about landscape influence. The system then rigorously evaluates these ensembles by testing their out-of-sample predictive power against independent movement or demographic data not used in the fitting process. This process identifies not a single 'true' resistance map, but a bounded set of ecologically plausible and predictive landscapes, explicitly quantifying uncertainty in the inference.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework Validating Structural Equation Models of Road Impacts
ideas.description: This research integrates Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) into a formal causal inference pipeline to move beyond correlation. The approach begins by constructing a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) to explicitly articulate and justify the assumed causal relationships among road impact mechanisms and ecological outcomes. SEM is then used to estimate the strength of these pathways from observational data. Crucially, the causal estimates from the SEM are tested and validated using quasi-experimental designs, such as leveraging natural gradients or planned infrastructure changes in a BACI framework, to assess whether the modeled relationships hold under conditions that better approximate causal identification.

ideas.title: A Falsifiable Framework for Parsimonious Mechanism Attribution in Road Impacts
ideas.description: This research establishes a methodology to rigorously test whether a road impact is best explained by a single dominant mechanism or requires a multi-mechanism model. The approach begins by identifying the most plausible primary mechanism and constructing a predictive model based on it. The model's residuals are then analyzed for systematic patterns that could only be explained by secondary, interacting mechanisms, using causal inference techniques to avoid confounding. The final output is a parsimonious, evidence-based attribution that is robust against both oversimplification and unnecessary complexity.

ideas.title: Modeling Confounded Landscape Resistance as a Mechanistic Inference Problem
ideas.description: This research direction posits that the inherent spatial heterogeneity in resistance within a single land cover class (e.g., forest of varying structure near vs. far from a road) is systematically influenced by multiple, confounded road impact mechanisms. By explicitly modeling within-class resistance as a spatially varying surface predicted by covariates related to different mechanisms (e.g., noise gradients for avoidance, traffic volume for mortality), and treating standard study designs as providing confounded observational data, the approach uses spatial statistical methods (e.g., confirmatory path modeling or variance partitioning) to infer the relative strength of each mechanism. This moves beyond simply improving corridor prediction to formally testing mechanistic hypotheses from imperfect field data, turning a limitation of study design into an analytical opportunity.

ideas.title: Calibrating Expert Judgment: A Meta-Analytic Framework for Reliability Conditions
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from clinical psychology, where the reliability of expert diagnostic judgment is not assumed but systematically quantified through psychometric meta-analysis. The analogous problem—determining when clinical diagnoses are reliable—is solved by aggregating studies of inter-rater reliability and predictive validity to identify moderator variables (e.g., use of structured instruments, clinician experience). Adapted to the original idea, the research would systematically gather and meta-analyze existing studies where expert-derived resistance values (e.g., microbial antibiotic resistance, pest pesticide resistance, material stress resistance) were later validated with experimental data. The analysis would quantify the conditions—such as the expert's sub-field alignment with the problem, the complexity of the resistance mechanism, the availability of reference data for calibration, and whether judgments are individual or aggregated—that serve as predictors of accuracy, thereby moving beyond a blanket statement on reliability to a conditional, evidence-based framework.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Biomarker Monitoring to Individual Animal Road Ecology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from clinical medicine, where intensive longitudinal monitoring of biomarkers in individual patients is used to pinpoint the mechanistic effects of drugs or diseases. The analogous problem is understanding the precise, individual-level impact of an environmental stressor (roads) on an organism's state. The adapted research idea is to treat individual animals as 'patients' and roads as the 'treatment/stressor.' By deploying a suite of biologging sensors to create a continuous 'clinical panel' for each animal—measuring physiological stress (e.g., heart rate, glucocorticoids), fine-scale movement paths, energy expenditure, and location—researchers can directly measure the acute and chronic effects of road proximity and crossing events on survival proxies, movement modification, and stress physiology. This individual-based mechanistic approach moves beyond population-level correlations to establish causal pathways and individual susceptibility, much like a clinical trial establishes a drug's effect and side-effect profile in human subjects.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Resolution Climate Modeling for Continental Habitat Connectivity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical, multi-resolution modeling framework from climate science to the problem of scaling HSM-inferred resistance models for continental connectivity assessments. In climate modeling, a global circulation model (GCM) runs at coarse resolution to simulate planetary-scale dynamics, and its outputs are used to force higher-resolution regional climate models (RCMs) for specific areas. Analogously, we propose developing a continental-scale HSM-inferred resistance surface at a coarse resolution (e.g., 10-50 km) to identify continent-wide connectivity patterns and major pinch points. The outputs from this macro-scale model—specifically, the predicted flow and accumulated cost—would then be used as dynamic boundary conditions to initiate and constrain finer-resolution (e.g., 100 m - 1 km) HSM-inferred connectivity models for priority regions. This nested approach makes the computational challenge tractable while ensuring that local, high-resolution assessments are informed by and consistent with the broader continental context.

ideas.title: Treating Model-Inferred Resistance as a Null Hypothesis
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard paradigm in antimicrobial resistance (AMR) prediction, where expert opinion is often the starting point or final arbiter. Drawing an analogy from evidence-based medicine's approach to evaluating new diagnostic tests, we treat the output of a data-driven ML model as the default, baseline prediction (akin to a new diagnostic test). Expert opinion is then positioned as the 'gold standard' intervention whose additive value must be empirically demonstrated. The adapted research idea is to design a rigorous, prospective evaluation where model-inferred resistance profiles are the initial null hypothesis. The study systematically identifies scenarios where expert review is triggered and quantitatively measures whether this review changes the prediction in a way that improves accuracy or clinical outcomes compared to the model-alone baseline. This frames expert input not as a necessary foundation, but as a potentially valuable—but costly—resource to be deployed selectively based on evidence of its marginal utility.

ideas.title: Calibrating Landscape Resistance with Movement Data via Inverse Modeling
ideas.description: This idea proposes to validate and calibrate HSM-derived resistance values using a solution analogous to inverse modeling in hydrology. In groundwater studies, initial estimates of hydraulic conductivity are adjusted to fit observed water head data. Here, we adapt this by using an individual-based movement or genetic simulation model to generate predictions from an initial HSM resistance surface. An inverse modeling routine is then employed to iteratively adjust the resistance values across the landscape to minimize the discrepancy between the model's predictions (e.g., simulated movement corridors or genetic distances) and empirical data from animal GPS tracks or population genetics. This provides a rigorous, data-driven method to test and refine inferred resistance values, moving from hypothetical suitability to validated landscape permeability.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference Trials: Using Mitigation Outcomes to Quantify and Correct Researcher Bias
ideas.description: This research designs controlled, multi-arm trials where diverse teams of researchers, selected for differing theoretical backgrounds, are presented with an ambiguous system exhibiting multiple plausible causal mechanisms. Each team must propose and implement a specific mitigation strategy based on their inferred mechanism. The subsequent success or failure of each mitigation in a controlled environment serves a dual purpose: first, as Bayesian evidence to statistically infer the true operating mechanism (the inverse problem), and second, as ground-truth data to rigorously quantify the correlation between researcher background and the accuracy of their mechanistic inference. This creates a meta-scientific tool for mapping epistemic bias and developing debiasing protocols for complex systems analysis.

ideas.title: Multi-Dimensional Harm Integration for Robust Mitigation Prioritization
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to systematically identify and integrate multiple, often competing, dimensions of catastrophic harm—including mortality, chronic morbidity, loss of critical functionality, and cascading societal disruptions—into a single, transparent prioritization model. It employs structured expert elicitation and cross-domain scenario analysis to define a weighted harm ontology, avoiding the bias inherent in optimizing for any single metric like mortality reduction alone. The output is a dynamic decision-support system that visualizes trade-offs and generates mitigation portfolios robust across the full spectrum of potential harms, moving beyond a simple inversion of prior bias.

ideas.title: Mechanism Space Specification (MSS) and Automated Consistency Audit Framework
ideas.description: This framework requires authors to formally define a 'Mechanism Space'—a structured, machine-readable document enumerating all plausible causal mechanisms for their phenomenon, along with their testable empirical signatures. Authors must submit this specification alongside their data and code. Automated audit tools then verify the analytical workflow, checking if the reported analyses sufficiently test the predictions of all enumerated mechanisms or justify the exclusion of any. This creates an enforceable standard for multi-mechanism inference, moving it from a recommendation to a verifiable component of reproducible research.

ideas.title: Mechanistic hypothesis testing via cross-validated, multi-kernel individual-based movement simulation
ideas.description: This framework transforms resistance surfaces from a static model parameter into a source of testable, mechanistic movement hypotheses. Instead of directly parameterizing a single IBM, the HSM-inferred landscape is used to define alternative behavioral kernels (e.g., resistance as a bias in turning angles vs. a modulator of step length) within an individual-based simulation platform. A rigorous cross-validation protocol, where models are trained on one subset of empirical trajectories and used to forecast held-out movements, competitively evaluates these kernels. The outcome is a movement model that not only fits data but also identifies the most predictive behavioral mechanism and can be generalized to novel landscapes or scenarios.

ideas.title: A spatially adaptive, regionally stratified framework for continental connectivity modeling
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the flaw of spatial non-stationarity by abandoning a single, continental-scale HSM. Instead, it employs an objective, data-driven stratification of the continent into coherent ecological regions. Within each region, a locally parameterized HSM infers a resistance surface that accurately reflects regional species-environment relationships. These regional surfaces are then seamlessly integrated using boundary-harmonization techniques, producing a final connectivity assessment that is both continentally consistent and locally ecologically valid.

ideas.title: A Triangulation Framework for Assessing the Reliability of Expert-Derived Values in Ground-Truth-Ambiguous Domains
ideas.description: This research develops a formal framework to assess the reliability of expert-derived quantitative judgments in domains where objective ground truth is unavailable or contested. Instead of measuring accuracy, the system triangulates three reliability dimensions: predictive success against available empirical benchmarks, convergence across methodologically diverse and independent expert elicitations, and the structural soundness of the documented reasoning process. The output is a composite reliability index that identifies the specific conditions—such as problem structure, expertise diversity, and elicitation protocol—under which expert-derived values are most robust for decision-making, even in the absence of definitive verification.

ideas.title: Iterative Bayesian Calibration of Resistance with Conflict Diagnostics
ideas.description: This system implements a transparent, cyclical Bayesian framework to formally integrate expert-elicited resistance priors with likelihoods from Habitat Suitability Model (HSM) inversion. The key innovation is an embedded conflict diagnostic that quantifies divergence between information sources, triggering targeted data collection or expert re-elicitation. Each cycle produces a testable posterior map, which is validated against independent movement or genetic data, and then used to inform the next round of expert elicitation or model refinement, creating a self-correcting, evidence-driven workflow.

ideas.title: Triple-Blind Adversarial Collaboration to Quantify and Control for Inferential Bias
ideas.description: This research system creates a controlled, multi-stage environment to isolate and measure the influence of researcher background on mechanistic inference. An independent steering committee first generates experimental or observational datasets where multiple causal explanations are plausibly supported. Researchers from different theoretical backgrounds are then randomly assigned to advocate for a specific mechanistic explanation, which may not align with their known prior work. Their analyses and arguments are evaluated by a separate, blinded referee panel, whose own decision patterns are subsequently audited for bias. This closed-loop, triple-blind design rigorously separates the phenomenon being studied from the biases of the investigators studying it.

ideas.title: Model landscape resistance as a heterogeneous composite property using sub-grid parameterization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniform resistance within a land cover class by drawing an analogy from climate science, where the uniform property assumption within model grid cells was solved using sub-grid parameterization. The analogous solution involves representing a coarse grid cell as a mosaic of 'tiles' with properties modified by local conditions. Adapted to landscape ecology, this means modeling the resistance of a single land cover polygon or raster cell as a heterogeneous composite. The resistance value for any point within that class is calculated by a function that integrates high-resolution spatial data on modulating factors—such as topographic roughness, vegetation density from LiDAR or satellite indices, soil moisture, and distance to linear features like roads or trails. This creates a spatially explicit, variable resistance surface even within a nominally homogeneous land cover class, moving beyond a single categorical resistance value to a mechanistic, environmentally graded one.
