ideas.title: Active Causal Falsification via Designed Sequential Interventions
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem as one of active causal discovery rather than passive inference. A research system is designed to propose a set of plausible generative mechanisms for a phenomenon and then algorithmically designs a sequence of real-world mitigation experiments, each with multiple intervention arms. The system analyzes the pattern of successes and failures across this sequence not to confirm a single mechanism, but to systematically falsify incompatible causal models. By focusing on falsification through strategically chosen interventions that yield divergent predictions under different models, the method overcomes the equifinality problem and converges on a robust, empirically validated mechanism.

ideas.title: Parameterizing Agent-Based Movement with HSM Resistance as a Social Force
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from statistical physics and active matter research, where macroscopic field data is used to parameterize 'social forces' in individual-based particle models. The analogous solution involves deriving virtual forces from observed fields to govern agent acceleration. Here, we adapt this by treating the HSM-inferred resistance surface not merely as a static cost grid for probabilistic path selection, but as a dynamic potential energy field. The negative spatial gradient of this resistance field generates a virtual force that directly influences the acceleration and directional decisions of simulated individual animals (agents). This force-based approach, integrated with the agent's internal state, creates a fine-scale, mechanistic movement model where trajectories emerge from the physics-like interaction between the individual and the landscape, offering a novel bridge between population-level resistance estimates and the mechanistic simulation of individual movement.

ideas.title: Blinded Mechanism Evaluation in Computational Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of blinding from clinical trial design to test for author inference bias in scientific research. The analogous problem in clinical research is preventing bias in treatment efficacy assessment, solved through double/triple-blind protocols where the treatment identity is concealed. The adapted research idea involves designing experiments where researchers evaluate datasets or model outputs generated by known but hidden mechanisms. By blinding the evaluators to the origin (e.g., which research group proposed it) and even the label of each candidate mechanism, the experiment can isolate and measure the influence of researcher background and pre-existing theoretical allegiance on their inferred explanation when the data underdetermines the conclusion. This creates a direct, controlled test for cognitive bias in mechanistic inference.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Resistance Surfaces with Covariate-Informed Intra-Class Variance
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a simple uniform resistance assumption by modeling resistance within a land cover class as a distribution rather than a single value. The system integrates spatially continuous global covariate layers (e.g., spectral indices, topography) within a hierarchical Bayesian model to predict local resistance deviations from the class mean. This structure provides a rigorous, data-driven method to capture heterogeneity while maintaining model identifiability and allowing for uncertainty propagation. The resulting resistance surfaces are both more ecologically realistic and statistically robust, improving transferability and interpretability.

ideas.title: A Bayesian Multi-Mechanism Belief Network Framework for Formalizing and Comparing Mechanistic Hypotheses
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Bayesian belief networks with a multi-mechanism inference philosophy to develop a formal framework for representing, comparing, and updating expert-derived mechanistic hypotheses. The core innovation is a structured belief network where nodes represent not only uncertain parameters but also discrete candidate mechanisms and the key evidence that discriminates between them. This allows researchers to formally encode expert priors on both parameters and the plausibility of entire mechanistic pathways, then use a unified Bayesian updating procedure to compute posterior probabilities for each mechanism given the data. The result is a rigorous methodology that moves beyond simply acknowledging alternative explanations to providing a quantitative, evidence-based ranking of their relative consistency with available observations, thereby guiding focused experimental design to resolve remaining ambiguities.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference of System Mechanism via Intervention Response
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem-solving approach from systems pharmacology, where a drug's mechanism is deduced from the system's response to it and to other pathway blockers. The core idea is to use the success or failure of specific, targeted mitigation measures as diagnostic signals to infer which underlying mechanism was actually operating in a complex system (e.g., a social, economic, or ecological system). By designing a portfolio of interventions that theoretically should succeed or fail under different mechanistic hypotheses, and then observing the real-world outcomes, one can apply statistical inference methods (like Bayesian model selection) to the pattern of responses. This transforms mitigation efforts from purely applied actions into active probes for fundamental system understanding, directly addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension by using practical, scaled interventions to reveal abstract, systemic mechanisms.

ideas.title: Inferring Landscape Resistance from Genetic Connectivity via Inverse Network Tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from network and medical tomography to solve the inverse problem in landscape genetics. The analogous field is Network Tomography, where internal network link properties (like resistance) are inferred statistically from end-to-end probe measurements between external nodes. Here, genetic samples are the nodes, and pairwise genetic differentiation is the observed connectivity pattern. By developing or applying an inverse model (e.g., a Bayesian optimization or machine learning framework), we can directly infer the resistance values assigned to landscape features (like land cover, elevation) that are most consistent with the observed genetic data, moving beyond the traditional approach of testing pre-defined resistance surfaces.

ideas.title: Adversarial Convergence Protocol for Meta-Inference Bias Auditing
ideas.description: This system operationalizes bias correction through a structured adversarial workflow. Independent, blinded teams first propose competing frameworks for quantifying inference bias in a given literature review. These proposals are then subjected to cross-critique and revision in a series of structured rounds. A pre-registered algorithmic arbitrator assesses the convergence of the independently revised estimates, producing a final bias-corrected synthesis that is robust to the preferences of any single meta-researcher or team.

Expert knowledge is then formally integrated not as a blanket overlay, but as a set of modular, context-specific corrections—such as priors for rare mechanisms or constraints based on biological plausibility—applied only to pre-identified failure domains. The final system is a calibrated hybrid where the model provides the scalable prior, and expert input acts as a validated, traceable corrective layer, ensuring robustness and preventing the perpetuation of baseline model errors.

The system automatically monitors for significant divergence between model predictions and observed outcomes, triggering recalibration protocols that may involve expert re-engagement, model structure adjustment, or hyperparameter tuning. This produces a self-correcting expert system that maintains formal rigor while reducing reliance on static, potentially biased judgments.
